coredata.dc:title: Biogenic synthesis of argentum nanocomposites for visible light photocatalyst of dye degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Kavitha, G.
Vinoth kumar, J.
Pavithra, S.
Komal, M.
Sherlin Nivetha, M.
Kayalvizhi, R.
Abirami, N.
coredata.dc:description: In the current study, Silver-Activated carbon (Ag-AC) nanocomposites were synthesized by a green method using the leaf extract of Baccaurea motleyana and activated carbon obtained from the dried seeds of Baccaurea motleyana. The biomolecules present in the Baccaurea motleyana act as a complexing as well as a capping agent. The size and band gap energy of the synthesized Ag-AC nanocomposite were found to be 4.5 nm and 2.1 eV, respectively. Methylene Blue (MB) and Rhodamine B (RhB) is a widely used dye which is detected both in wastewater and in sewage treatment plant effluents. The synthesized Ag-AC nanocomposite shows an invincible photocatalytic property in the degradation of dyes and textile waste water under Xenon light. The results showed that 88.02 % of MB dye and 80.74 % of RhB. Additionally, the textile effluent treatment discovered that the Ag-AC catalyst degraded up to 93.42 % within 150 min and prominently decreased the levels of certain physicochemical parameters of textile effluents. The degradation of both the dyes follows a pseudo-first-order reaction and the rate of the constant (k) of photodegradation was found to be 0.0218 min−1 and 0.0137 min−1 for MB and RhB dye. This study reveals that the synthesized Ag-AC nanocatalyst is an excellent photocatalyst for the photodegradation of dyes and textile wastewater due to the synergistic effect of Ag nanoparticles in photocatalysis and activated carbon in adsorption of pollutants. The Ag-AC nanocomposite can be recycled and used for upto 4 cycles without any significant alteration in the photocatalytic activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Textile effluents
Baccaurea motleyana
Capping agent
Nanocatalyst
Synergistic effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of Baccaurea motleyana leaf extracts
Ag NPs synthesis by using Baccaurea motleyana leaves
Photocatalytic degradation of synthetic azo dyes
Photo catalytic activity of Ag-AC nanocomposites

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM images of (a) Ag-NPs (b) Ag-AC nanocomposite (c) AC (d, e) elemental mapping of resulting Ag-AC nanocomposites exhibiting the presence of Ag, C and (f) EDX of Ag-AC.
HR-TEM images of Ag-AC nanocomposite at (a) 10 nm scale, (b) SAED pattern (c) lattice spacing, (d) Ag-AC nanocomposite at 100 nm, and (e) Particle size histogram of Ag-AC.
XRD images of AC, Ag and Ag-AC nanocomposites.
(a) The optical property of Ag, AC, Ag-AC, (b) band gap of AC, (c) band gap of Ag-AC.
BET isotherm plot; (a) N2 adsorption desorption of Ag-AC and (b) BJH pore size distribution of Ag-AC.
(a) Raman Spectra of Ag: Inset of (a) AC spectra, (b) Raman spectra of Ag-Ac nanocomposite.

Enhanced photo catalytic efficiency of MB dye by (a) Ag and (b) Ag-AC catalysts, MB's photo degradation efficiency; inset of (c): illustrates MB's colour intensity reduction (d) Pseudo first order Kinetics model of MB degradation, RhB dye photo catalytic performance with catalysts (e) Ag (f) Ag-AC. (g) Pseudo-first-order Kinetics diagram showing RhB degradation. (h) Photo catalytic degradation efficacy of RhB dye; (h) inset: colour intensity decline of RhB dye. (i) Possible mechanism of Ag- AC nanocomposites.
The effect of scavengers upon efficient photo catalytic degradation of (a) MB and (b) RhB over an Ag-AC nanostructure.

(a) UV–vis spectra of Ag-AC photocatalyst based textile wastewater degradation; (b) degradation % of textile effluent; inset of fig: (b) illustrate the decrease in textile effluent colour intensity; (c) demonstrate the Ag-AC catalyst's mineralization efficacy for RhB, MB, and Textile Effluent.
Investigation of reusability of Ag-AC catalyst for photocatalytic degradation of textile effluent.

Mass spectrum pathway obtained for the photo-catalytic degradation of MB under UV irradiation in the presence of Ag-AC nanocomposite at different time intervals. (a) Before degradation, (b) Dark, (c) 5 mins, (d) 15 mins, and (e) 30 mins, (f) 60 mins.

Mass spectrum pathway obtained for the photo-catalytic degradation of RhB under UV irradiation in the presence of Ag-AC nanocomposite at different time intervals. (a) Before degradation, (b) Dark, (c) 15 mins, (d) 30 mins, and (e) 60 mins, (f) 90 mins.
Photo-catalytic degradation pathway of MB using Ag-AC nanocomposite.
Photo-catalytic degradation pathway of RhB using Ag-AC nanocomposite.
Evaluation of physicochemical factors before and after treatment.

head.ce:title: Biogenic synthesis of argentum nanocomposites for visible light photocatalyst of dye degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Engineering and Technology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur 603203, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: SRM Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Engineering and Technology, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Kattankulathur 603203, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the current study, Silver-Activated carbon (Ag-AC) nanocomposites were synthesized by a green method using the leaf extract of Baccaurea motleyana and activated carbon obtained from the dried seeds of Baccaurea motleyana. The biomolecules present in the Baccaurea motleyana act as a complexing as well as a capping agent. The size and band gap energy of the synthesized Ag-AC nanocomposite were found to be 4.5 nm and 2.1 eV, respectively. Methylene Blue (MB) and Rhodamine B (RhB) is a widely used dye which is detected both in wastewater and in sewage treatment plant effluents. The synthesized Ag-AC nanocomposite shows an invincible photocatalytic property in the degradation of dyes and textile waste water under Xenon light. The results showed that 88.02 % of MB dye and 80.74 % of RhB. Additionally, the textile effluent treatment discovered that the Ag-AC catalyst degraded up to 93.42 % within 150 min and prominently decreased the levels of certain physicochemical parameters of textile effluents. The degradation of both the dyes follows a pseudo-first-order reaction and the rate of the constant (k) of photodegradation was found to be 0.0218 min−1 and 0.0137 min−1 for MB and RhB dye. This study reveals that the synthesized Ag-AC nanocatalyst is an excellent photocatalyst for the photodegradation of dyes and textile wastewater due to the synergistic effect of Ag nanoparticles in photocatalysis and activated carbon in adsorption of pollutants. The Ag-AC nanocomposite can be recycled and used for upto 4 cycles without any significant alteration in the photocatalytic activity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the span of decades, the world has seen amazing economic growth. Water and harmful chemical consumption have increased as a result of agricultural growth, industrialization, and urbanization. This massive rise resulted in widespread plant and soil infestations, and also the ingestion of rivers and lakes. The defilement of water is a long-standing dispute which has increasingly deteriorated in the last ten years. Because of the rising levels of water pollution, wastewater treatment is receiving a lot of considerable interest. Utmost textile effluents encompass multiple harmful and destructive pollutants, including phenols, that are highly harmful to life forms, industrial wastes out from metals, petrochemical, foodstuffs, and pharmaceutical industries are substantial bases of contamination. The reformation of such kinds of issues, as well as the development of vital precautionary trials, generally receipts a long time.

The most primitive signs of water contamination, such as stench in the water and industrial waste disposal, were observed in densely populated extents. The use of dye in a multitude of sectors, including electronics, confectionery, pulp, fabrics, skincare, coatings, and pharmacology, results in annual production of 2 million tonnes of dye. Around 15 % of textile industry dyeing effluent emits fecal contamination effluents, posing a major threat to the environment and human health. Roughly 10,000 different colors are often used in the garment industry today, and the volume of wastewater produced is enormous [1–3]. Due to its low-cost synthesis, [4] substantial toxicity, [1] superior chemical stability, [5,6] rapid bioaccumulation, and color diversity, dyeing effluent from synthetic dyes drastically raise pollution levels when compared to natural dyes [7–9]. When discharged to water, may cause substantial harm to marine life. Breast cancer, dermatitis, hypersensitivity, cardiovascular illness, congenital defects, liver and kidney damage, and ventilatory ailments are all caused by dye wastewater [10–13]. As a result, those dyes must be removed from textile effluent until it is released into the aquatic system.

To date, coagulation-flocculation, oxidation/reduction, and adsorption have all been utilized to remediate these pollutants [14–17]. Adsorption has been the most efficient and prospective strategy. Because of its ease of use, cost-effective, lack of secondary contaminants, and accessibility of adsorbent materials [3,18]. In photocatalytic activity, activated carbon also played a significant role. The phyto-compounds bonded to the surface of AC assisted in the activation of photon across valence band to conduction band and improved the catalytic performance of nanostructured materials by adsorbing organic contaminants from the surface of the compound (dye/drug). Numerous studies were conducted to look into the use of agro by-products or industrial effluent in the fabrication of commercial activated carbon as an environment conscious solution to the crisis. Due to their wide surface area, sorption capacity, and mesoporosity, activated carbon (AC) is commonly used in the adsorption process.

Activated carbon, on the other hand, offers significant benefits in low-concentration wastewater purification. Activated carbon may adsorb a wide range of organic compounds primarily through weaker molecular interactions (van der Waals forces), especially dispersion forces. Due to their particular features ascribed to a high surface-to-volume ratio, nanocarriers have significant catalytic performance toward the synthesis of various organics. In such an air atmosphere, though, nanoparticles are incredibly unstable and can quickly agglomerate to reduce their surface energy, resulting in a considerable drop in their catalytic efficiency [19]. As a result, metal NPs anchored on high surface carbonaceous material have captivated scientists’ curiosity, as they improve nanoparticle stability and catalytic performance [20,21]. Silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) in the instance have excellent catalytic and optical properties and are used in metallurgical, fuel, and sensing areas because they are less expensive than some other noble metals [22–24]. Organometallic bridging, membrane filtration, and thermal condensation followed by reduction are the ways used to make carbon-supported Ag NPs [25]. To achieve immaculate synergy in between carbon surface and metal precursor, all approaches involve a precise tuning of carbon surface properties [26,27]. The Ag NPs have also been produced by reducing metal salts by minimal plant extracts, dried fruits, and green, with Phyto constituents acting as a reduction and capping agent [28–32]. Leaf extract, fruits, amino acids, and vegetables, on the other hand, are the ideal feedstocks for the formation of carbon-based nanomaterials such as nanosheets and activated charcoal. Citric acid, for instance, is a suitable reducing and stabilizing agent in the synthesis of nanomaterials, as well as an effective source of carbon in the hydrothermal fusion of nanostructured materials [33,34]. A green synthesis method is one of the natural resources available to eradicate harmful chemicals from water. When compared to other methods, this method's efficiency is great and it can be utilized in a variety of circumstances. For instance, conventional procedures typically require costly chemical reagents and tend to produce solid or liquid wastes that require further investments for disposal and treatment. In this regard, additional criteria related to sustainability, such as technical-environmental (like low risk for operators and economic considerations costs of raw materials and manufacturing). These additional criteria include relatively short reaction times, low temperatures, and environmentally friendly synthesis [35–38].The biosynthesis of carbon-supported nanostructured materials in a step process can be performed by adjusting reaction parameters such as carbon precursor concentration, metal salt consolidation, temperature, and reaction rate.

Pranwadee Kaewmuang et al., synthesized NiO particles in the presence of ethylene glycol by solvothermal-calcination process for the photo catalytic degradation of Congo red with 76 % degradation efficiency [39]. Mohammad Mehdi Sabzehmeidani et al., fabricated NiO/ZnO composite nanofibers for photodegradation of binary mixture of rhodamine B and methylene blue. The maximum photodegradation of RhB and MB using Zn was 59.41 and 65.43 % [42].

Silver nanoparticles were synthesized by Chandra Kishore et al., using marine algae for the catalytic degradation of methylene blue. The outcome showed that Ag NPs performed as a better catalyst, entirely degraded MB in 20 min [41]. Activated carbon (AC) plays an significant role in the photo catalytic degradation process. Activated carbon adsorbed the organic pollutants from the surface of compound (dye/drug) and the Phyto-compounds attached on the surface of AC helped in the excitation of electron from valance to conduction band and enhanced the catalytic activity of nanocomposites. The noble metal decorating or coating on the activated carbon surface increased the surface area, increased the production of free radicals, and prolonged the stability of the e-h pair. In comparison to other catalyst materials, these characteristics build the total degrading activity.

Carbon assisted nanomaterials have recently piqued the interest of researchers due to their superior structure and reliability compared to unsupported nanomaterials. Nano carriers, such as Ag NPs play a vital role in the biological and environmental research. The Ag Nps are fabricated in this study using a sustainable reaction conditions with leaf extracts of Baccaurea motleyana (a member of the Phyllanthaceae family) as reducing and stabilising agents. To make hybrid nanocomposites, prepared nanoparticles were anchored on activated carbon synthesised from Baccaurea motleyana seed, and it was examined by UV–vis, XRD, Raman, BET, SEM, EDAX, and TEM analysis. The catalytic utilization of all synthesized samples was also investigated for the degradation of cationic dyes: MB and RhB, as well as textile effluent.

The materials were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, India, which included commercial silver nitrate salt (AgNO3), ethanol, sulphuric acid (H2SO4), NaHCO3, Rhodamine B, Methylene Blue. The solvents and compounds used in this research were all research-grade and hadn't been purified in any manner. Distilled water was used throughout the observation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of Baccaurea motleyana leaf extracts
ce:section.ce:para: A sufficient proportion of Baccaurea motleyana leaves (100 g) were obtained from the nursery garden. Baccaurea motleyana leaves were rinsed with ultrapure water. The leaves were dried in the shade. 10 g of dried leaves powder was added to 100 ml of DI water and refluxed for 45 min at 40 °C. The extracted mixture was then strained through Whatman No. 42 filter paper and kept at 4 °C for additional testing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ag NPs synthesis by using Baccaurea motleyana leaves
ce:section.ce:para: For green synthesis, 20 ml of Baccaurea motleyana leaves was stirred with AgNO3 (80 ml, 1 mM) solution and held at 45 °C. The formation of a yellow - orange solution with the flow of time revealed the green synthesis of Ag NPs. The solid residue from the NPs solution was rinsed with water, continued by ethanol to eliminate any impurities, and dried at 70 °C for 6 h to acquire nano-particle powder. Then, the powder was dried and stored for future research.

Fruits of Baccaurea motleyana were collected from the hilly areas of Kodaikanal, Tamil Nadu, and India. In which the seeds were cleansed, completely dried, and pulverised in a crusher. Samples were again dried at 120 °C, and the recovered powder was combined with 1:1.5 wt proportion of conc. H2SO4 and left in an oven at 110–150 °C for overnight. The emerging char was completely rinsed with distilled water, then by a 2 % NaHCO3 solution till the effervescences terminated, and let to remain in a 2 % NaHCO3 solution overnight. After that, the acid-treated Baccaurea motleyana seed powder was collected washed thoroughly with distilled water until it was free of bicarbonate, and left to dry at 105 °C. Baccaurea motleyana seed was activated for half an hour in an autoclave then they are thermally activated for 2 h at 800 °C in a furnace to form activated carbon [35].

1 g of Ag NPs was dissolved in 50 ml of DI water and agitated for one hour at room temperature on a mechanical stirrer to form a homogenous solution. Afterwards, 0.5 g of AC was mixed towards the Silver solutions and ultra-sonicated for 20 min to achieve an even more uniform dispersion. The samples were centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 15 min with deionized water. The resulting mixture was dried at 80 °C and grinded into a fine powder in a mortar and are used for photo catalytic degradation studies.

A UV–visible spectrophotometer was used to determine the composite's optical characteristics (Shimadzu UV–vis 160).With an XRD2 - Philips X'pert Modular Powder Differactometer, XRD patterns were obtained to evaluate the phase and structure of the nanocomposite. FESEM imaging was used to investigate the composite's surface morphology (Carl ZEISS Sigma 300 VP). The TEM equipment is used to analyse crystalline structures in samples with adequate resolution (JEOL JEM-1400 120 kV). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using a PHI 5000 VERSA PROBE II to identify the chemical surface composition of the nanomaterial. Micro Raman Spectrometer (LabRAM HR Evolution Raman spectroscopy equipped with a 633 nm solid-state laser) was used to capable of probing the chemical composition of materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of synthetic azo dyes
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activity of fabricated nanomaterials was measured on cationic dyes such as rhodamine B (RhB) and methylene blue (MB). The catalyst (Ag - AC) was added to 60 ml of 10 ppm dye solutions at a concentration of 15 mg per 60 ml. The mixture was constantly agitated in dark for half an hour to ensure that equilibrium among dye molecule adsorption and desorption on the surface of the catalyst was reached. After that, the reaction was left in the visible light for 90 min. 5 ml dye samples were injected for every 30 min to assess the intensity of test solution using a UV–vis spectrophotometer. To determine the by-products and intermediates of RhB and MB deterioration, Samples from various time intervals are collected and examined using a (LC-MS) liquid chromatography mass spectrometry. Subsequently, quenchers with a concentration of 1 mmol/L were added to the
photo catalytic solutions of RhB and MB. The light absorption measurements of the mixtures with quencher were acquired on exposure.

Textile effluent was collected in a textile industry in Tirupur, Tamil Nadu, India, which primarily focuses on dyeing, printing, and finishing. At constant temperature, the textile effluent is a dark blue color. The effluent utilized in the experiment was not treated in any way in the industry. The research was conducted with textile wastewater using nanocomposites. 100 mg of Ag-AC was mixed in a 500 ml wastewater sample. The test solutions were stirred for half an hour in the darkness to achieve the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. The solutions were then exposed under visible light for 150 min while being constantly stirred. Physiochemical parameters such as pH, BOD, EC, COD, and TDS were investigated. Titrimetric procedures were also used to assess the chlorides, sulphates and hardness of non-treated and treated textile waste water samples.

SEM analysis was used to further explore the morphology of the Ag-AC nanocomposite. SEM images of Ag, Ag-AC nanocomposites, and AC are shown in Fig. 1 a–c. AC has a conglomerate surface (disruption in surface morphology), and the surface roughness implies a large surface area, which favours dye adsorption arising in the aqueous phase. Ag NPs were agglomerated whereas an Ag-AC nanocomposite has a spherical morphology. The elemental composition of the Ag-AC was further validated by EDX spectroscopy Fig. 1 f. The EDX spectra revealed the key peaks, such as carbon (C) and silver (Ag) and Fig. 1 (d, e) shows the results of EDX elemental mapping. The Ag-NPs were uniformly distributed across the surface of the functionalized activated carbon, according to the results. It was also shown that activated carbon may efficiently promote consistent encapsulation of Ag-NPs while causing no contamination on its surface. The TEM images Fig. 2 a–d indicate the shape and size of an Ag-AC composite, while the SAED pattern shows their spatial features. The inclusion of Ag NPs in the composite of the biomass can be shown in Fig. 2 d. A few spherical shapes have been found in the TEM images of the Ag-AC composite, which is owing to the biosynthesis of Ag NPs in the carbon matrix. The presence of concentric rings in the SAED pattern of synthesized AgNPs shows that they are polycrystalline in nature were shown in Fig. 2 b. The d-spacing values were derived from the HR-TEM image of Ag-AC nanocomposite were displayed in Fig. 2 c. In addition, the average particle size of the Ag-AC was observed to be ∼ 4.5 nm using a Gaussian distribution, as shown in Fig. 2 e. The computed d-spacing values were found to be 0.20 nm, which corresponds to the (200) plane of Ag NPs face-centered cubic (FCC) structure.

The crystalline phase of Ag-AC nanocomposite, Ag NPs were identified by XRD patterns, as illustrated in Fig. 3 . The greater intensity peaks (111) in the XRD spectra specify the effective materialization and crystal size of Ag NPs. The intensity maxima 2θ values at 38.30, 44.29, 64.57, 77.37, and 81.52, which corresponds to the (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) planes, demonstrate the (FCC) face-centered cubic feature of Ag NPs. The XRD peaks for AC samples Fig. 3 showed a large peak in the range of 22.18, which might be ascribed to the hexagonal carbon-like replications reflected from the (002) lattice plane. These large peaks are a feature of amorphous carbon, which has a disordered stacking of carbon rings. The average crystal particle size of Ag-AC nanocomposite was calculated by using the Debye-Scherrer equation and XRD data. In Ag-AC NC, the average Ag crystallite size was determined to be 7.5 nm.

In Fig. 4 a shows the UV–vis spectra of Ag, AC and Ag-AC nanocomposite. Due to the obvious Surface plasmon resonance (SPR), Ag NPs exhibited an absorbance band in the wavelength of 390 nm, while the phytochemicals contained in the leaf extracts produced a broad absorbance band at 320 nm. The bands due to the plant extract are also visible in this spectrum, demonstrating that Ag NPs were effectively capped with natural molecules. Ag NPs shows a red shift. The AC had a UV cut-off in the ∼ 220 nm range, which could be attributable to light emission of electrons from valence to the conduction band or the π–π* shift of carbon sp2 (CC). In the Absorption spectra of the Ag-AC nanocomposite, a minor hypsochromic shift can be seen, which is ascribed to a coupling effect of Silver with activated carbon, constitutive composite synthesis. The direct band gap can be estimated using Tauc plot, which proves that optical absorption intensity is proportional to photon energy and band gap. As (hʋ)2 = A (hʋ − Eg), where A is the slope of the Tauc plot in the linear region and, α is the absorption coefficient, h is the Planck's constant, Eg is the optical band gap, and the Tauc plot of AC and Ag-AC nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 4 (b,c), and the computed band gaps are 3.1 eV and 2.1 eV, respectively.

The surface area and porosity of the nanocomposite are determined by the BET results. The surface area of Ag-AC nanocomposite was determined using N2 adsorption behaviour and the BET prototype, as well as the porosities (PSD) using the conventional BJH technique. The measured surface area of the Ag-AC nanocomposite was observed to be 28.138 m2g−1, that is nearly equivalent to the surface area of a single point. The hysteresis loops with in N2 adsorption as well as desorption isotherm that represents the Type-IV isotherm, is depicted in Fig. 5 a. The hysteresis loop confirmed that composite is porous in existence (d = 1.53 nm). The Fig. 5 b depicts the PSD graph of a nanocomposite. Such findings indicate the ease with which dye molecules and textile effluent could be accommodated in the porous structure of a Ag-AC nanocomposite. As a result, it demonstrated improved photo catalytic properties and adsorption for both the removal of textile effluent and dye molecules.

XPS study was used to further evaluate the elemental identification. The XPS spectra of C1s, and Ag 3d are shown in Fig. 6 . The main components of Ag-AC nanocomposite were C and Ag. The spectral data of the Ag 3d peak show that two components exist side by side in the Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 signals at 368.42 eV, and 374.40 eV are for Ag+ respectively. Two deconvolution peaks in C1s spectral data correspond towards the OCO (carboxyl, 288.53 eV), CO (hydroxyl, 284.80 eV) groups, respectively.

In Fig. 7 shows the Raman spectra of Ag-NPs. 237, 467, 688, 1052, 1446, and 1523 cm−1 are the vibrational modes occurs in this system. Baccaurea motleyana, which also acts as a surfactant for Ag-NPs. Numerous organic constituents, such as hydroxyl and carboxylic groups, were present in the extract. The band at 237 cm−1 in Raman spectra is owing to AgO stretching mode. The stretching vibration of CNC and CSC causes the vibrational peaks at 467 and 688 cm−1. The anti-symmetric CO stretching vibration of the carboxylic group produces some other band at 1523 cm−1. The Raman spectra of Ag-AC nanocomposites of biomass-derived carbon material shows two peaks at around 1342 cm−1 and 1584 cm−1, which have been classified as D-band and G-band, respectively, along with a absorption peaks of Ag occurs around 237, 1523 and 1052 cm−1 confirms the formation of Ag-AC nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photo catalytic activity of Ag-AC nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para:
We also examined the catalytic efficiency of the photo catalyst when exposed to visible light. For the photo degradation experiment, 15 mg of Ag, Ag-AC photo catalyst were added to the MB and RhB solutions separately. While photo catalysts were mixed with the MB and RhB solutions, the absorption maxima dropped significantly as the irradiation period was increased. The absorption bands at 650 nm and 550 nm are due to the formation of MB dye and RhB dye were totally vanished within 60 and 90 min, respectively, and the solution turns colourless Fig. 8 a & b). The dye degradation performed a pseudo-first order kinetics could be described as follows:
(1)
ln
(
C 0
/ C t )
=
k t

Where C0 and Ct represent the intensity or concentration of Rhodamine B and MB dye before and after deterioration, and ‘k’ would be the rate constant. Appropriately, the photo degradation rate constants (k) for MB and RhB dye were found to be 0.0218 min−1 and 0.0137 min−1, respectively. The following equation was used to calculate the photo degradation percentage efficiency:
(2)
%
d e g r a d a t i o n
=
[ ( C 0 - -
C ) / C 0 ]
x
100

Where C0 and Ct are the dyes absorbance or concentrations during degradation. The percentage of degradation efficiency of Rhodamine B and MB dye was calculated, and under visible light irradiation, 88.02 % of MB dye and 80.74 % of RhB are deteriorated by Ag-AC catalyst shown in Fig. 8 e–f. It could infer that the hybrid heterojunction has the optimum degradation rate. Because of the less photon-induced electron–hole recombination and enhanced visible-light absorption. Ag-AC had a significantly greater degrading efficiency because to its increased surface area, smaller particle and crystallite sizes, and superior optical activity. The result demonstrates that by trapping photoelectrons, Ag nanoparticles improve charge separation. Due to Surface Plasmon resonance, Ag serves as an e− sink, reduces the rate of recombination, increases quantum yield, and increases the ability to absorb visible light. The elimination of methylene blue, rhodamine B, environmental remediation, and wastewater treatment are all possible uses revealed by the photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposites as they have been created. The reduced recombination of electron-hole pairs, the increased photocatalytic activity in the composite, and the nanoparticles' size, shape, and crystal structure may all be attributed. The many adsorption sites on the surface of activated carbon materials might enhance the catalytic activity. Hot electrons were produced by the metal trapping, which could improve the ability to degrade. The noble metal decorating or coating on the activated carbon surface increased the surface area, increased the production of free radicals, and prolonged the stability of the e-h pair as shown in Fig. 8 i. In comparison to other catalyst materials, these characteristics build the total degrading activity. We also looked into the Ag-AC catalyst’s radical scavenger experiment. The scavengers screening for MB and RhB pollutant degradation was performed to detect the reactive species for efficient photo catalytic activity, and the findings are displayed in Fig. 9 a & b. It is obvious from the trapping studies that h+ species are the primary reactive species in the degradation reaction. Furthermore, IPA and BQ play a limited part in the degradation process. During the degradation process, only a minimal quantity of O2• and OH• species were generated. The experiment with elemental trapping was shown that the h+ radicals are the main radicals in the dye degradation process when exposed to visible-light.

In Fig. 10 depicts how the absorption of a deteriorated textile wastewater solution changes over time in the existence of Ag-AC nanocomposite when exposed to visible-light. At 150 min, the peak at 420 nm decreased by around 93 %. Although the Ag-AC nanocomposite eliminates the color of industrial effluents, the total elimination of hazardous components and mineralization must still be verified. As a result, TOC and COD studies validated the mineralization of textile effluents. The physicochemical properties of obtained effluent and treated wastewater, including pH, total suspended solids (TSS), total dissolved solids (TDS), biological oxygen demand (BOD), conductivity, turbidity, and intensity of real industry wastewater before and after were tested and listed in Table.1 .

Photo-catalysis seems to be another disruptive technology that results in the complete mineralization of some of the organic contaminants. In aqueous suspensions, catalytic degradation of Methylene Blue was examined utilizing Ag-AC nanocomposite as the optimum catalyst under light irradiation. Ag-AC were capable of degrading MB dye, exhibiting complete mineralization of (C) carbon, (N) nitrogen, and (S) sulphur heteroatoms into NH4 +, NO3, CO2, and SO4 2− respectively, in addition to removing the dye's colour. At various time intervals, the intermediates formed during the degradation reaction was examined using LC-MS. The primary aromatic metabolites produced by the MB destruction process have been identified. They are shown in Strategy 1 , where they would be systematically revealed in lowering molecular weight. The two oxidative agents are quite well notable as the initiators of the degradation process: the photo-produced holes h+ (primarily involved in the OH• radicals and/or decarboxylation reaction (‘Photo-Kolbe’) that are identified as highly active and degrading, although non-selective intermediates and could be developed by the reactions listed below. In the CS+C functional group, the conceived OH• radicals attack MB. As an outcome, the cleavage of the bonds of the CS+C functional group in Methylene Blue can be related to the first step of MB disintegration. The sulfoxide would be the first substance resulting from the degradation of MB (m/e = 303). The sulfoxide group reacts with an OH• radical to form the sulfone (which is undetectable) and opens the benzene ring. First, the core amino group is cleaved at the NC double bond. As seen for products with m/e = 303, 230, and 218, the H• radicals were used to saturate the two remaining amino bonds, yielding a substituted aniline. The aromatic substituted aniline CN cleavage from the second step into 3-hydrosulfinyl-N, N-dimethyl aniline. Another CN cleavage leads to the formation of benzenethiol. Because of their hydrophilic nature, many more hydroxylated intermediates formed as a result of their poor extractability were difficult to detect.

Considering the Ag-AC-assisted photocatalyst system extremely high dye degradation rate, research was conducted to identify potential degradation intermediates. In UV irradiation, it was found that RhB dye was remarkably stable in the absence of photo-catalysts. The photo-induced hydroxyl radicals, (•OH) might damage the central carbon of RhB as a result of photocatalytic degradation of Rh-B, effectively decolorizing the dye before it was further broken down by the N-de-ethylation process. The potential product, including 9-(2-carboxyphenyl)-6-(ethylamino)-3H-xanthen-3-iminium, were produced during the degradation of the aforementioned compound by the cleavage of CN bond. The further fragmentation the formation of 9-(2-carboxyphenyl)-6-(ethylamino)-3H-xanthen-3-iminium respectively as a result of breakdown of the CN bond. The resultant intermediates undergo consecutive progressive degradation through bond cleavage to produce the desired end product of Rh-B photocatalytic degradation, which is 8-phenyl-1H-dicyclopenta. It is clear from the chemical results and visual inspection that the azo (CN) bond in the RhB has entirely broken down into a number of compounds. The fragmentary compounds are colourless because they lack chromosphere such azo bonds. The compounds' structures that were discovered as a result of RhB photocatalytic degradation, the suggested route is depicted in Strategy 2 . Notably, as demonstrated by LC-MS analysis, the RhB dye was completely mineralized after undergoing photocatalytic degradation when exposed to UV light, demonstrating the system's potential for use in the construction of large-scale photocatalytic reactors. Utilizing the m/z values of the mass spectral data from the LC-MS study, it was possible to predict the predicted structures of the main intermediate compounds during the degradation process.

In summation, a green, simple, and cost-efficient process was used to successfully synthesize an Ag-AC nanocomposite. The described technique is simple to perform at room temperature. A stable Ag-AC nanocomposite was developed by the well-assembled Ag on the surface of AC, as shown by the analytical techniques. The computed band gap value of the synthesized Ag-AC composite, which is expected to boost photocatalytic activity, is 2.1 eV. The synthesized Ag-AC nanocomposite's photocatalytic capability was demonstrated for the MB dye and RhB degradation under UV light irradiation at room temperature under atmospheric circumstances. The findings of photocatalytic studies showed that Ag-AC composite had a greater photocatalytic activity for the degradation of MB degradation efficiency about 88.02 %) and for RhB (degradation efficiency of about 80.74 %). Additionally, the textile effluent treatment discovered that the Ag-AC catalyst degraded up to 93.42 % within 150 min and prominently decreased the levels of certain physicochemical parameters of textile effluents. The synthesized Ag-AC composite contains AC, which is essential for improving the photocatalytic activity of the Ag-AC nanocomposite through improved dye adsorption and more photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Additionally, the functions of AC are crucial to the photocatalytic degradation process. Functional groups on the AC surface function as electron reservoirs that efficiently prevent photogenerated electron-hole pair recombination. Overall findings point to a promising photocatalyst for the degradation of harmful organic dyes. This highly photoactive, environmentally friendly Ag-AC nanocomposite is anticipated to support the use of photocatalysts in real-world applications and offer an easy, efficient method for creating complicated, elevated catalysts for pollutant removal [40].

G. Kavitha: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. J. Vinoth kumar: Data curation. S. Pavithra: Software. M. Komal: Formal analysis. M. Sherlin Nivetha: Visualization. R. Kayalvizhi: Data curation. N. Abirami: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science and Technology, Tamil Nadu - 603 203, India, for providing the necessary facilities to complete the research work.
